coredata.dc:title: Room-temperature upcycling of agricultural waste to carbon dots and its application as a selective fluorescence nanoprobe for iron (III) analysis in diverse samples

coredata.dc:description: A smart, green, practical, and scalable method has been developed for the upcycling of orange peels into carbon dots (C-dots) by room-temperature alkali treatment for the first time. The prepared C-dots showed excellent fluorescence emission at 428 nm upon excitation at 333 nm. As well, the C-dots exhibit high water stability and aqueous solubility, and a tiny particle size of 1.74 ± 0.44 nm. Based on the selective quenching of the synthesized C-dots fluorescence by Fe3+, a new fast and green fluorescence probe has been validated for the determination of Fe3+over the concentration range of 1.0–400.0 µM with a detection limit of 0.2 µM (11.2 ppb). The selectivity of the sensor has been established over various metal ions and insecticides. The developed sensor has been utilized for the determination of Fe3+ in different environmental water samples, such as river, irrigation, and tap water, with an excellent recovery % (99.08–108.24 %). Besides, the new probe has been applied for Fe3+ determination in ampoules with an average recovery % of 99.86±1.15. The environmental impact of the new method has been positively confirmed by ComplexGAPI and AGREE metrics. Furthermore, the method's blueness has been estimated by the blue applicability grade index (BAGI), and the obtained high score (70) demonstrates the high practicability of the method. The method whiteness has also been assessed by the RGB 12 algorithm, which gives a whiteness parameter of 92.5 that highlights its practicality, greenness, and analytical performance. Besides, the presented approach is the first to valorize agricultural waste by room-temperature treatment, a merit that opens the door for broad applications in limited-income laboratories.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Rania El-Shaheny: received her B.Sc., M.Sc., and Ph.D. degrees from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt. She is currently a professor at the Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University. She was a visiting researcher at the Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, from 2018 until 2022. She was awarded the Takeda Science Foundation International Fellowship, Mansoura University Encouragement Award, and Mansoura University Excellence in Science Award. Her research focuses on the development of new luminescence-based probes for clinically significant compounds in addition to developing probes for pharmaceuticals and environmental pollutants. Her research interests also included the use of nanomaterials such as nano extraction beds and nanoprobes for pharmaceuticals. Furthermore, her research included developing novel analytical techniques for investigating the safety, pharmacokinetics, and in vivo transformations of drugs.
This paper is based upon work supported by the Academy of Scientific Research and Technology (ASRT), Egypt , under grant number (Green Fund_ 202219495 ).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A smart, green, practical, and scalable method has been developed for the upcycling of orange peels into carbon dots (C-dots) by room-temperature alkali treatment for the first time. The prepared C-dots showed excellent fluorescence emission at 428 nm upon excitation at 333 nm. As well, the C-dots exhibit high water stability and aqueous solubility, and a tiny particle size of 1.74 ± 0.44 nm. Based on the selective quenching of the synthesized C-dots fluorescence by Fe3+, a new fast and green fluorescence probe has been validated for the determination of Fe3+over the concentration range of 1.0–400.0 µM with a detection limit of 0.2 µM (11.2 ppb). The selectivity of the sensor has been established over various metal ions and insecticides. The developed sensor has been utilized for the determination of Fe3+ in different environmental water samples, such as river, irrigation, and tap water, with an excellent recovery % (99.08–108.24 %). Besides, the new probe has been applied for Fe3+ determination in ampoules with an average recovery % of 99.86±1.15. The environmental impact of the new method has been positively confirmed by ComplexGAPI and AGREE metrics. Furthermore, the method's blueness has been estimated by the blue applicability grade index (BAGI), and the obtained high score (70) demonstrates the high practicability of the method. The method whiteness has also been assessed by the RGB 12 algorithm, which gives a whiteness parameter of 92.5 that highlights its practicality, greenness, and analytical performance. Besides, the presented approach is the first to valorize agricultural waste by room-temperature treatment, a merit that opens the door for broad applications in limited-income laboratories.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (C-dots) are a type of carbon-based nanomaterial having intriguing characteristics and great potential for numerous applications. C-dots exhibit superior properties relative to semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes with respect to water solubility, chemical inactivity, easy modification, photostability, and safety. Additionally, C-dots show good biocompatibility, enabling their use in bioimaging, drug delivery, and biosensors. Furthermore, the excellent electronic characteristics of C-dots grant them great potential as catalysts and optronics, and their superior photoluminescence properties endow them with wide applications as sensors [1–9]. Since their discovery in 2004, many synthetic routes have been applied for the synthesis of C-dots, implementing either a top-down or bottom-up approach [10,11].

Lately, green C-dots that are synthesized from natural sources, such as plants, biomass, and agricultural wastes, have drawn the attention of scientists all over the world [2,4,12–14]. Natural resources are renewable, cheap, eco-friendly, and easily accessible. Therefore, the synthesis of green C-dots has become a leading sustainable attitude globally due to their effective conversion of low-value or valueless substrates into higher-value products. Additionally, some researchers used the biomass synthesized carbon dots for analysis of environmental pollutants, including heavy metals (e.g., Pb2+), pesticides (e.g., pendimethalin and indoxacarb), and fungicides (e.g., Metalaxyl) [15,16]. Besides, the synthesis of C-dots from bio-wastes plays a prominent role in the control of environmental pollution [12]. Thus, this study aims to develop a new, simple, economical, and easily implemented method for the preparation of green C-dots from orange peels and their use for environmental control and pharmaceutical analysis of the heavy metal (Fe3+) in various matrices.

Orange (Citrus sinensis) is a type of citrus fruit that is considered one of the most consumed fruits worldwide. It is cultivated in many areas all over the world. In 2022/2023, the main producers of oranges include Brazil, China, the European Union, Mexico, and Egypt. That year, the total worldwide production volume of oranges was 47.77 million metric tons. This data shows the global abundance of orange production and consumption. In the same vein, orange is the main citrus crop in Egypt, representing 70 % of the total cultivated citrus area. Egypt’s production of oranges reached 3.7 million metric tons. Constituents of orange peel are sugars, e.g., glucose and fructose (23 %), cellulose (22 %), pectin (25 %), and hemicellulose (11 %) [17]. With these components, the valorization of orange peels by using them as a feedstock for the synthesis of C-dots is reasonable. Recently, valorization of food wastes, by-products, and biomasses has significantly gained a higher value. This approach remarkably contributes to the implementation of sustainability and circular economy concepts by decreasing the use and consumption of materials, enhancing waste management for the creation of further value, and protecting the environment from the bad impacts of waste. In this study, we elaborated on a new simple technique for the first time: synthesizing C-dots simply by impregnating orange peel powder with alkali at room temperature without any energy consumption. The synthesis procedure excludes the use of any special instruments which guarantee its application in chemistry laboratories with limited resources. This method furnished fluorescent C-dots with high stability, water solubility, and excellent fluorescence characteristics with large Stokes shifts (about 90 nm). These properties qualify the C-dots as a fluorescence probe for rapid determination of Fe3+ in different matrices.

Iron is among the most essential elements for life, where it is vital for oxygen transport, enzymatic reactions, and DNA synthesis [18]. Iron is formulated in dosage forms that are administered to treat iron-poor anemia that affects billions of people globally. Nevertheless, high levels of Fe3+ in water resources are a major threat to human health. The high amount of Fe3+ in the human body leads to numerous health consequences, for example, cancer of many organs. Industrial activities such as mining and steel production are the main sources of Fe3+in environmental water and wastewater. Besides, drinking water may be contaminated with Fe3+due to rust in water pipelines and building materials utilized in the drinking water resources [19]. As a result, iron is more abundant than other heavy metals in water sources and different environmental waters. Therefore, it is fundamental to monitor the level of Fe3+ in drinking and environmental water keenly.

Analytical methods used for the determination of Fe3+ include the electrochemical method [20], electrospray ionization mass spectrometry [21], high-performance liquid chromatography [22], colorimetric analysis [23,24], and fluorimetry [25,26]. Nevertheless, these methods have constraints such as lengthy time, expensiveness, complicated apparatus, tough experimental settings, necessity for skilled experts, and the use of plentiful organic solvents and hazardous reagents. Hence, it is highly recommended that facile, economic, green, accurate, and rapid techniques be executed for the recognition of Fe3+in different matrices, such as environmental water and pharmaceutical ampoules.

With this background, this research targeted the accurate determination of Fe3+ in tap water and different environmental water samples, such as river and irrigation water, utilizing a nanosensor based on the synthesis of green C-dots from orange peels by an energy-free strategy via room-temperature alkali treatment. Furthermore, the accuracy, fastness, and simplicity of the method encouraged us to apply it for quality control of Fe3+in ampoules. Performing the entire synthesis process at room temperature via instrument-free methodology and using a widely available fruit waste are extra merits that offer wide applicability and accessibility to the developed method. Consequently, it is very suited for application in laboratories with low income.

A fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies, Santa Clara, USA) equipped with a Xenon lamp was used with a 1 cm quartz cell. The fluorescence spectra were scanned at 600 nm/min, 800 V sensitivity, excitation, and emission monochromators slit width = 5 nm. A Shimadzu Spectrophotometer (Kyoto, Japan) was used to scan UV–VIS absorption spectra. A water bath with a thermostat (Cambridge Ltd, UK), a 2–16P centrifuge (Germany), and an SS 101 H 230 Eumax ultrasonic bath (USA) were utilized. Measurements of pH were done using an NV P‐901 pH meter from Consort (Belgium). CAMAG® UV Cabinet with double wavelength 254/366 nm (2 × 8 W) was utilized to study the photostability of the prepared C-dots.

Orange fruits were purchased from a market in Mansoura, Dakahlia, Egypt. The solvents and chemicals used were of analytical grade. Quinine sulfate, FeCl3·6H2O, FeSO4·7H2O, HgCl2, NiCl2·6H2O, CuCl2·2H2O, Zn (NO3)2, CdSO4, Pb (NO3)2, NaOH, HCl, H2SO4, and H3PO4 were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich Co., U.S.A. Acetic acid and sodium acetate trihydrate were bought from Adwic Co. (Cairo, Egypt). Acetate buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.0) was prepared by adjusting the pH of sodium acetate (0.1 M) with acetic acid. A standard solution of FeCl3·6H2O (1.0 mM) was prepared in distilled water. Sacrofer® ampoules (100 mg iron/mL), a product of Amoun Pharmaceutical Co. (Obour, Al-Qalyubia, Egypt), were purchased from a local Egyptian Pharmacy.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-dots from orange peels
ce:section.ce:para: Orange fruits were thoroughly washed several times with distilled water and peeled off. The orange peels have been dried and ground into a fine powder with a laboratory grinder. Five g of the orange peel powder was accurately weighed and mixed with 100 mL NaOH solution (1.25 M). The mixture was then vigorously mixed until the powder was impregnated with NaOH solution and left at room temperature for 2 hrs. The brown supernatant was collected, filtered with Whatman filter paper followed by a 0.22 µm syringe filter, and neutralized with HCl (1.0 M). The obtained solution was centrifugated at 6000 rpm for 30 min to eliminate any suspended particles. The supernatant was aspirated and kept in brown sealed bottles at 4 °C. The prepared C-dots were submitted for characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Depiction of the properties of the prepared C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Features of the prepared C-dots have been studied using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) utilizing a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) operated at 200 kV. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) evaluation for the scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Oxford X-Max 20) was conducted for elemental investigation. Further chemical state and elemental composition investigation was performed by means of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) with a PHI VersaProbe III (Al 1486.6 eV mono at 25.2 W X-ray source). For survey spectra measurements, a pass energy of 224 eV was used, while for high-resolution elemental analysis, 27 eV was used. For XPS, the samples were prepared as follows: the C-dots colloidal dispersion was drop cast on a 5 × 5 mm2 silicon wafer and dried under ambient conditions, then immobilized by a double-sided carbon tape on a 25 mm XPS sample holder. Data analyses were performed using MultiPak version 9.8.0.19. All spectra were charge-corrected using the C1s signal for adventitious carbon at 284.8 eV. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) from Thermo-Fisher Scientific Nicolet—iS10 was applied to explore the functional groups of the C-dots. A study of the absorbance and fluorescence spectra of the C-dots has been performed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of C-dots fluorescence response to different metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: The ability of the prepared C-dots as a selective fluorescence nanosensor for determination of Fe3+ has been studied as follows: 100 µL of C-dots solution were mixed with a suitable volume of individual metal ions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Ni2+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, and Cd2+) to get a final metal ion concentration of 50 µM, and 1 mL of Britton Robinson Buffer (pH 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, or 8.0). The volumes were completed to 10 mL with distilled water, and the mixtures were well-mixed. Blank experiments have been done simultaneously. The fluorescence intensity of the prepared solutions was measured at 428 nm upon excitation at 333 nm. A blank experiment has also been done. All experiments were done in triplicate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Calculation of the relative quantum yield of the prepared C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The relative quantum yield (Ф) of the prepared C-dots was estimated, employing quinine sulfate as a standard fluorophore of a known Ф=0.54 in 0.1 M H2SO4, using the following equation: Ф x = ФSt

Where Ix = the integrated fluorescence for C-dots, Ist = the integrated fluorescence for quinine sulfate, Ax = absorbance of C-dots, As = absorbance of quinine sulfate, and n = the refractive index of the solvent [27]. In both experiments, for C-dots and quinine sulfate, the solvent is water with n = 1.33.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ in standard solution and calibration curve
ce:section.ce:para: Into a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks, increasing quantities of Fe3+ standard solution were added to prepare final concentrations of 1.0- 400.0 µM and mixed with 1.0 mL of acetate buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.0) and 100 µL C-dots. The solutions were completed to final volumes with distilled water and thoroughly mixed. The determination of fluorescence intensity (I) was carried out at 428 nm after excitation at 333 nm. A blank experiment was done by omitting Fe3+, and its fluorescence intensity (I0) was measured. Figuring the calibration graph has been done by plotting Ln(I0/I) against the concentration of Fe3+ [µM], and the regression equation has been obtained. Triplicate experiments were performed for each concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Fe3+ in different water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The river water sample was collected from the Nile River (Talkha City, Mansoura, Egypt), and the irrigation water sample was obtained from a canal in Shrinqash village, Dakahlia, Egypt. A tap water sample was collected from our laboratory. All water samples were filtered with 0.45 µm syringe filter to eliminate any debris and particles. A volume of 3.0 mL of each water sample has been added to a group of 10 mL volumetric flasks, followed by 1.0 mL of acetate buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.0) and 100 µL C-dots. The final volumes were made up of distilled water and then mixed. For spiking experiments, each type of water sample (3.0 mL)has been fortified with Fe3+at four different concentration levels (10.0, 20.0, 30.0, and 50.0 µM), and the described steps have been repeated. Each experiment has been performed in triplicate. The fluorescence intensity (I) was measured at 428 nm after excitation at 333 nm. The concentrations of Fe3+ [µM] have been calculated from the calibration curve.

Into a set of 10 mL volumetric flasks, 100.0 µL aliquots of C-dots and 1.0 mL aliquots of acetate buffer (0.1 M, pH 5.0) were transferred. Then, different volumes from Sacrofer® ampoules were added to prepare five different concentrations (50.0, 100.0, 200.0, 300.0, 400.0, 500.0 µM) of iron. Afterward, the same procedure mentioned under Section “2.7″ was followed, and the nominal contents of iron in ampoules were determined using the corresponding regression equation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Room-temperature synthesis of C-dots from orange peels
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis procedure of C-dots from orange peels has been optimized by investigating the influence of different concentrations of NaOH and the influence of alkali impregnation time. The effect of the strength of NaOH has been studied over the concentration range 1.0 M to 5.0 M. The best quantum yield was obtained using 1.25 M NaOH. Furthermore, the influence of impregnation time has been studied over 1–24 h. The best quantum yield was achieved after incubating orange peel powder with NaOH for 2 h, while a further increase in time had no enhancement effect on the quantum yield. Thus, 2 h has been selected as the best time for optimum synthesis of the C-dots. The process of synthesis of the C-dots by the new approach is illustrated in Fig.1 .

Mixing NaOH with water generates significant heat that may trigger the carbonization process. This methodology is validated by the findings of Zheng et al. [28] and Li et al. [29], which pertain to the room-temperature production of C-dots by NaOH treatment of small molecules. The production of C-dots can be ascribed to the degradation of orange peels' constituents, including glucose, fructose, cellulose, and hemicellulose, in the presence of NaOH. As per Krochta et al. [48], the polysaccharides derived from agricultural products degrade into organic carboxylic acids in an alkaline environment. Thus, the polysaccharide components of orange peels, including cellulose, a glucose polymer, and hemicellulose, a copolymer of glucose, galactose, mannose, and xylose, break down into carboxylic acids. The combination of organic carboxylic acids and NaOH subsequently experienced nucleation and aromatic growth, resulting in the formation of C-dots without the need for external heating or energy.

The suggested method not only addresses the environmental struggles linked to agricultural waste but also functions as an energy-free, green, fast, cost-effective, and simple approach for C-dots synthesis. The developed approach manifests a circular economy model by upcycling orange peels as a C-dots synthesis substrate rather than chemical substrates. This reduces raw material dependency, extends the product life cycle, and enriches value. Besides, the developed approach merits room temperature synthesis that significantly saves energy and money and corroborates the practicality, sustainability, and applicability in limited-income laboratories.

The prepared C-dots are well distributed in water. The obtained TEM image (Fig. 2a ) of the C-dots revealed uniform rounded particles with particle size within the range of 0.9 to 2.7 nm, and a mean particle size of 1.74 ± 0.44 nm (Fig. 2b). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) chemical mapping has been done using energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The obtained result proved the existence of C (69.22 %), oxygen (21.03 %), sodium (4.75 %), and chlorine (4.78 %) (Fig. 2c). Infrared spectroscopy analysis has been performed to elucidate the functional groups at the C-dots surface. As demonstrated in Fig. 2d, the FTIR spectrum of the C-dots displayed a strong peak band at 3349 cm-1, reflecting the OH stretch (H-bonded). The band at 2920 cm-1 is attributed to CH stretching of alkanes, while the band at 1589 cm-1 is ascribed to C = C and/or C = O stretching [30]. The strong peak at 1041 cm-1 is ascribed to CO stretching, while the peak at 1407 cm-1 is attributed to OH bending of the carboxylic group. The FTIR spectrum also shows the C–Cl wag in the fingerprint region [30].

Elemental analysis of the prepared C-dots was investigated by XPS. The survey spectrum in (Fig. 3a ) shows that the C-dots consist mainly of carbon and oxygen with 70.2 and 28.2 atomic %, respectively. Trace amounts of Na, Cl, and N can also be detected with very low content (not >1.6 atomic % in total). The high-resolution XPS analysis of the C1s region can be fitted into three main peaks at 284.8, 286.3, and 288.3 eV corresponding to CC, CO, and COO [31–33], respectively (Fig. 3b).

The high-resolution spectrum of the O1s region can be fitted into three peaks at 531.3, 532.5, and 535.9 eV (Fig. 3c). The first two peaks can be attributed to CO and C = O, respectively [34]. However, the peak at 535.9 could be due to the KLL signal of Na ions [35,36].

The absorption spectrum of the C-dots prepared from orange peels (Fig. 3d) revealed two bands at 200 and 282 nm that indicate the π-π* transition of the sp2 conjugated carbon C = C of the C-dots’ aromatic structure. This result agrees with the literature about C-dots [10,37], where C-dots are known to have optical absorption in the UV region with a slight extension in the visible range.

Photoluminescence is a fascinating property of C-dots. The π-π* transition on the aromatic structure is responsible for the fluorescence emission of C-dots. Besides, the oxygenated functional groups on the carbon structure are the principal surface states of C-dots that contribute to surface state fluorescence emission [38]. Thus, we carried out fluorescence scanning to explore this characteristic of the prepared C-dots. The C-dots synthesized from orange peels exhibited strong blue-green fluorescence peaking at 428 nm upon excitation at 333 nm (Fig. 3d). The Quantum yield of the prepared C-dots has been calculated relative to quinine sulfate standard, and it was found to be 7.5 %, indicating its good fluorescence. Furthermore, the prepared C-dots demonstrated excitation-dependent emission when employing λex from 340 to 400 nm, as shown in Fig. 3e, while the emission wavelength is approximately constant upon varying the λex from 300 to 330 nm. On the other hand, a steady increase in the fluorescence intensity has been observed as the λex increased from 300 to 330 nm, and then a gradual decrease in the fluorescence intensity is experienced by increasing the λex from 340 to 400 nm. The reason for the unique phenomenon of excitation-dependent emission may be the optical collection of C-dots with dissimilar sizes (quantum size effect) or C-dots with dissimilar surface emissive traps. The distinction between particle size and photoluminescence emission is noted from the excitation-dependent emission spectra and the relatively broad emission band [37,39].

The stability of C-dots has been evaluated under a variety of conditions. C-dots were previously stored at 4 °C for a month to evaluate their stability by measuring their fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity of one-month-aged C-dots was maintained at 91 % of the fluorescence intensity of the fresh solution, which reveals the excellent stability of the C-dots (Fig. 4 ). Furthermore, the C-dots’ fluorescence was measured after being exposed to artificial daylight at room temperature for 6 h. The fluorescence of C-dots was stable and maintained at 95 % of its initial intensity. Furthermore, after exposing the C-dots to continuous UV radiation at 365 nm for 60 min, they retain about 80.5 % of their original intensity (Fig. 4). These findings reveal the stability of the prepared C-dots and support the feasibility of the application of C-dots derived from orange peels as nanosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: C-dots for sensing of Fe3+: optimization and validation
ce:section.ce:para: At first, the investigation of the effect of different pH values on the response of the prepared C-dots as a fluorescence sensor has been assessed by measuring the fluorescence quenching effect of different metal ions such as Hg2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Cd2+, Fe2+, and Fe3+ at different pH values (Fig. S1). It was observed that Hg2+ interferes with the determination of Fe3+ at pH 7.0–8.0, while Pb2+ interferes at pH 3. On the other hand, at pH five, all the metals other than Fe3+ did not significantly quench the fluorescence of C-dots. Thus, pH 5.0 is the optimum pH for the selective determination of Fe3+. The prepared C-dots showed a selective response to Fe3+ at pH 5.0, as demonstrated by the quantitative decrease of their fluorescence by increasing the concentrations of Fe3+ over the range of 1.0–400.0 µM (Fig. 5a ). Investigating different buffers of pH 5.0 such as Britton Robinson and acetate buffers reveals that the superiority of acetate buffer regarding reproducibility. For fully optimizing the experimental conditions for Fe3+determination, the volume of C-dots has varied from 0.25 to 1.25 mL. It has been found that 1.0 mL is the best volume for the highest fluorescence quenching by Fe3+ (Fig. S2).

The developed analytical method has been validated following the rules of the ICH Q2(R2) guidelines [40]. The linear relationship between Fe3+ concentration and C-dots fluorescence quenching (Ln I0/I) has been assessed across the working concentration range of 1.0–400.0 µM, utilizing nine concentrations. The regression line has been calculated (Fig. 5a), and the linear regression equation has been computed:
L n

)

The detection limit (DL) and quantitation limit (QL) have been determined from the standard deviation (SD) of the linear response (σ) and the slope (S), where DL = 3.3σ/S and QL =10σ/S. The DL was found to be 0.2 µM (11.2 ppb) and QL=0.6 µM.

Furthermore, accuracy was established over the linear concentration range. Excellent recovery % (99.0–101.0 %) is indicative of the high accuracy of the established method. The repeatability and intermediate precision of the method have been confirmed by triplicate analysis of three concentrations of Fe3+ within a single day and on three successive days, respectively. The relative standard deviation was ≤ 0.99 and 1.06 for intra- and inter-day precision, respectively. This indicates the good precision of the established method. A summary of the validation study of the developed method is displayed in Table 1 .

To investigate the selectivity anti-interference ability of the synthesized C-dots for Fe3+ sensing, different interfering species, including various cations (Fe2+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Ba2+, Na+, and K +), some anions (Cl-, SO4 2-, and acetate), and some insecticides that may exist in aquatic samples, including fipronil, dimethoate, fenoxaprop-p-ethyl (Fen), and malathion have been added to the C-dots, at final concentration of 1000 μM, in the presence and absence of Fe3+(150 μM). As can be seen in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence of C-dots is only significantly quenched in the presence of Fe3+. In addition, the signal of C-dots with Fe3+ was not affected by the presence of the tested interferent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of Fe3+by the synthesized C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
Experiments were performed to explore the underlying mechanism of quenching the native fluorescence of C-dots by Fe3+. Initially, the observed fluorescence of C-dots, which was measured after adding increasing concentrations of Fe3+, was corrected to exclude the effect of inner filtration. Inner filtration comes into action when the excitation or emission peak of the fluorophore (C-dots) intersects with the absorption peak of the quencher (Fe3+). The following equation has been applied [41]:
I
corr ected
=
I
obse rved
x
anti log
(
A ex
+
A em
2
)

Where Icorrected = the fluorescence intensity after correction by the exclusion of IFE, Iobserved = the fluorescence intensity as measured, and Aex and Aem = the absorbance of Fe3+ at the λexcitation and λemission of the C-dots, respectively.

The % suppressed efficiency ( %E) of the observed and the corrected fluorescence intensity has been estimated by the next equation [41]:
% E = [ 1 −
(
I
I
0
)
] x 100

A plot of %E for Icorrected and Iobserved (Fig. 6a ) revealed that the inner filtration is the main reason for quenching of the fluorescence of C-dots since the %E has dropped from 71 % to 17 % upon deletion of the inner filtration effect. This idea is supported by the fact that the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ significantly interferes with the excitation and emission spectra of C-dots (Fig. 3d)(); thus, inner filtration is expected to take place. Table S1 (supplementary material) shows the data used for inner filter effect calculations.

Next, Stern-Volmer analysis has been done at three rising temperature sets to recognize the impact of dynamic and static quenching [42] by applying the following equation:
I
0
I
= 1 +
K
s v
[
F
e
3 +
]

Where Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant and [Fe3+] is the molar concentration of [Fe3+]. Plots of I0/I versus [Fe3+] showed linear relation as shown from the values of correlation coefficient at different temperatures (Fig. 6b). Besides, the Stern-Volmer quenching constant is dependent on the temperature where it decreases as the temperature increases (Fig. 6b). This indicates that a static mechanism has a role in quenching of C-dots fluorescence by Fe3+. This is due to the formation of C-dots- Fe3+non-fluorescent complex; such a complex is cleaved at elevated temperatures, which causes the decrease of the Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

A proposal of the interaction mechanism between C-dots and Fe3+ is illustrated in Fig. 6c. The interaction occurs due to the binding sites available on the C-dots' surface because of the surface functionalities (-COOH and -OH). The interaction resulted in C-dot-Fe3+ complex formation. This complex formation enhanced non-radiative electron–hole recombination as electrons transferred from an excited state to the unfilled orbitals of Fe3⁺, resulting in fluorescence quenching [43].
Thus, this study unravels that the quenching of the native fluorescence of C-dots by Fe3+ is affected by a combination of inner filtration and non-fluorescent complex formation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application to tap, river, and irrigation water
ce:section.ce:para: According to the WHO [44], the mean iron concentration in rivers has been reported to be 0.7 mg/L, while the concentration of iron in drinking water should be <0.3 mg/L. However, high levels of iron in water may result from certain industrial activities like mining or rusting of water pipelines [45]. Human exposure to high amounts of iron leads to harmful symptoms like stomach pain, nausea, and vomiting. Besides, excess iron accumulates in internal organs, causing potentially fatal damage to the brain and liver. Thus, monitoring the iron levels in different water sources is mandatory.

The developed Fe3+-sensitive probe has been evaluated with real water samples like tap, river, and irrigation water. The experiments revealed the freedom of the three water samples from Fe3+, or it may exist at a trace concentration below the DL of the method. Thus, to validate the ability of the method for real water analysis, the water samples were spiked with known concentrations of Fe3+(10.0, 20.0, 30.0, and 50.0 µM). The high % recoveries, i.e., 102.15, 100.83, and 104.57 %, and the small values of SD (3.17, 1.16, and 3.14) in tap, river, and irrigation water, respectively, suggest that the C-dots derived from orange peels have a strong competence for the application as a fluorescence probe of recognition and quantitation of Fe3+ in real water. Table 2 contains a summary of the results of the determination of Fe3+ in the tested real water samples.

The sensitivity and selectivity of the developed sensor encouraged extending its applications to the determination of Fe3+ in ampoules. The excellent %recovery, along with a small standard deviation (99.86 ± 1.15 %), indicated the accuracy of the developed method and its applicability to quality control of iron in pharmaceuticals. A summary of the results obtained is displayed in Table 3 . Furthermore, the obtained results were validated by statistically comparing the obtained results with the results of a reported method that is based on the complexation of iron with piroxicam and measurement of the formed colored complex at an absorption maximum of 495 nm [46]. Student t-test and Variance ratio F-test revealed that there is no significant difference between the new method and the reported method regarding accuracy and precision, respectively [47].

Different recent approaches have been applied to comprehensively assess the performance of the developed analytical method in terms of environmental impact, practicability, and functionality [48]. First, two analytical greenness metrics have been utilized to estimate the environmental influence of the developed method [48–50]. Analytical GREEnness Metric [49] has been applied to evaluate different aspects related to green analytical chemistry (GAC), such as sample-related factors, number of steps, solvents and reagents used, energy and time needed, derivatization, and waste-related factors. Besides, the complementary green analytical procedure index (ComplexGAPI) [50] is specially designed to evaluate both the analytical method itself and the processes performed prior to the analytical procedure, such as nanomaterial synthesis. The results of AGREE (Fig. 7a ) and ComplexGAPI (Fig. 7b) prove the eco-friendly features of the method and its limited impact on the environment. The dependence on renewable bio-based reagents, minimum energy need, rapid analysis, high sample throughput, and the use of easily accessible instruments are the main green aspects of the developed method.

Second, the blueness of the developed method, which reflects its practicability, has been considered by applying the blue applicability grade index (BAGI) [51]. BAGI assesses the type of analysis, the number of analytes per run, the number of samples/times, the used reagents and equipment, the number of samples that can be concurrently treated, the necessity for preconcentration, the level of automation, the method of sample preparation, and the quantity of samples. The star-shaped BAGI pictogram (Fig. 7c) with a score of 70.0 highlights the practicality of the designed nanoprobe.

Third, the method has been assessed with respect to white analytical chemistry principles (WAC) utilizing the RGB 12 color model algorithm [52]. WAC is an expansion of GAC. WAC evaluates analytical methods regarding environmental impact (Green, G), analytical performance (Red, R), and practicality and feasibility of application (Blue, B) perspectives. The total whiteness parameter of the developed method is 92.5 %, with % R of 100 %, %G of 91.7 %, and %B of 85.7 % (Fig. 7d).
In summary, the results of this examination revealed the merits of the proposed method in terms of excellent analytical performance, minor environmental impact, and feasible applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparing the developed method and the reported C-dots-based literature for Fe3+determination
ce:section.ce:para: The newly developed method for Fe3+determination has been assessed in comparison with the reported literature that employed C-dots (Table 4 ). Most of the reported C-dots have been synthesized via a hydrothermal route that requires lengthy heating at very high temperatures up to 200 °C for several hours (2–30 h) [19,53–58] or microwave synthesis that consumes 700–1200 W energy [59,60]. Regarding sensitivity, our developed method is 3.5 to 68 times more sensitive than most of the developed C-dots-based methods for the determination of Fe3+ [19,53,56,57,60], and has a similar sensitivity to some of them [58,59]. On the other hand, some methods in the literature have higher sensitivity than our method [54,55]; however, these methods used toxic chemicals and a very high temperature autoclave for a long time for the synthesis of C-dots. In contrast, the suggested method does not spend energy during the synthesis process since it is accomplished at room temperature. Furthermore, the present method for C-dot synthesis eliminates the need for sophisticated specialized instruments, while the reported literature requires either an autoclave, an oil bath, or a microwave oven. Another significant asset for the developed method is its dependence on natural, cheap, and feasibly obtained orange peels as a plant waste substrate, in contrast to various reported methods that need chemical substrates like citric acid, 1-aminopropyl-3-methylimidazolium, hyaluronic acid, m-phenylenediamine, phenylalanine, ethylenediamine, and urea (Table 4). In turn, the advantages of the developed method are outstanding qualifying it for environmental monitoring of Fe3+.

A new, simple, practical, and efficient approach has been developed to produce C-dots from orange peels based on energy-free room temperature treatment with NaOH. The high fluorescence efficiency of the synthesized C-dots has been utilized to design a new fluorescence probe for the determination of Fe3+ in different environmental water samples from tap, river, and irrigation sources, besides commercially available ampoules. The excellent selectivity over other metal ions and high % recoveries (99.08–108.24 %) proved the ability of the method for environmental analysis. Different aspects concerning the environmental impact, analytical efficiency, and practicality of the new method have been positively assessed. The new method for C-dot synthesis is simple, energy-free, and utilizes renewable bio-based plant waste. This supports the global concept of a circular economy and GAC.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Heba M. Hashem: received her MSc from the Faculty of Science, Mansoura University, Egypt, and her PhD degrees from the Faculty of Science, Ain Shams University, Egypt. Her research interests involve the analysis of drug substances in dosage forms and biological fluids using electrochemical sensors and the analysis of environmental pollutants using fluorometric nanosensors.

Mahmoud El-Maghrabey: received his BSc and MSc degrees from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt, then received his PhD degree from the Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, Japan. He is an Associate Professor at the Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University. He was an Assistant Professor at the Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University. He received many scientific awards, including the Egypt State Encouraging Award, the Mansoura University Encouraging Award, and the Kyushu Analytical Chemistry Award for Young Researchers from the Japanese Society of Analytical Chemistry. His research focuses on the development of new mass spectrometric, fluorescence, and chemiluminescence-detecting probes for bioactive substances, including quinones, aldehydes, metals, pharmaceuticals, food allergens, and pollutants. Currently, he is focusing on developing non-enzymatic tags for immunoassays in addition to developing new nanosensors based on carbon nanomaterials and their application in pharmaceutical and environmental analysis.

Hadir Borg: was born in Mansoura, Egypt. She got her B.Sc. in Pharmaceutical Sciences in 2014 and then her M.Sc. in Pharmaceutical Sciences (Analytical Chemistry) in 2017 from Mansoura University. From 2014 to 2021, she was doing research focused on pharmaceutical analysis at Delta University for Science and Technology. Since 2021 until now, she has been studying for her PhD degree at Leibniz Univertsität Hannover in the research group of Prof. Dorfs and Prof. Bigall. Currently, her research interests are the synthesis and characterization of noble metal nanoparticle-based cryogels and their applications.

Galal Magdy: received his B.Sc. and M.Sc. from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt, then received his Ph.D. degree from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University, Egypt. He is currently an assistant professor at the Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Kafrelsheikh University. His research focuses on the development of new luminescence-based probes for clinically significant compounds in addition to developing probes for pharmaceuticals, environmental pollutants, food allergens, and additives. Besides, his research interests included the usage of nanomaterials as nanoprobes for pharmaceutical analysis, environmental analysis, and cell imaging.

Fathalla Belal received his MSc and PhD degrees from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt. He was a visiting researcher at the University of Shizuoka, Japan. He was Alexander von Humboldt's fellow in Germany. He is currently a professor of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry at the Faculty of Pharmacy at Mansoura University. His research interests involve the analysis of trace levels of biological substances and drugs using fluorescence probes and electrochemical sensors in addition to monitoring environmental pollutants. Recently, he focused on the synthesis of carbon and metal-based nanomaterials and their applications as nanosensors and nano-extraction beds.

Rania El-Shaheny: received her B.Sc., M.Sc., and Ph.D. degrees from the Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt. She is currently a professor at the Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry Department, Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University. She was a visiting researcher at the Graduate School of Biomedical Sciences, Nagasaki University, from 2018 until 2022. She was awarded the Takeda Science Foundation International Fellowship, Mansoura University Encouragement Award, and Mansoura University Excellence in Science Award. Her research focuses on the development of new luminescence-based probes for clinically significant compounds in addition to developing probes for pharmaceuticals and environmental pollutants. Her research interests also included the use of nanomaterials such as nano extraction beds and nanoprobes for pharmaceuticals. Furthermore, her research included developing novel analytical techniques for investigating the safety, pharmacokinetics, and in vivo transformations of drugs.
